From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [En masse](/w/index.php?title=En_masse&redirect=no "En masse"))

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article **may contain [original research](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research "Wikipedia:No original research")**. Please [improve it](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Glossary_of_French_words_and_expressions_in_English&action=edit) by [verifying](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability") the claims made and adding [inline citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Inline_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources"). Statements consisting only of original research should be removed. *(September 2019)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Many words in the English vocabulary are of [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") origin, most coming from the [Anglo-Norman](/wiki/Anglo-Norman_language "Anglo-Norman language") spoken by the [upper classes](/wiki/Upper_class "Upper class") in England for several hundred years after the [Norman Conquest](/wiki/Norman_conquest_of_England "Norman conquest of England"), before the language settled into what became [Modern English](/wiki/Modern_English "Modern English"). English [words of French origin](/wiki/List_of_English_words_of_French_origin "List of English words of French origin"), such as *art*, *competition*, *force*, *money*, and *table* are pronounced according to [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") rules of [phonology](/wiki/Phonology "Phonology"), rather than French, and English speakers commonly use them without any awareness of their French origin.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

This article covers French words and phrases that have entered the English lexicon without ever losing their character as Gallicisms: they remain unmistakably "French" to an English speaker. They are most common in written English, where they retain French [diacritics](/wiki/Diacritic "Diacritic") and are usually printed in italics. In spoken English, at least some attempt is generally made to pronounce them as they would sound in French. An entirely English pronunciation is regarded as a [solecism](/wiki/Solecism "Solecism").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Some of the entries were [never "good French"](/wiki/Franglais "Franglais"), in the sense of being grammatical, idiomatic French usage. Others were once normal French but have either become very old-fashioned or have acquired different meanings and connotations in the original language, to the extent that a native French speaker would not understand them, either at all or in the intended sense.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Used in English and French

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Glossary_of_French_words_and_expressions_in_English&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Used in English and French")]

### A

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Glossary_of_French_words_and_expressions_in_English&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: A")]

[à la](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%C3%A0_la "wikt:à la")
:   short for ([ellipsis](/wiki/Ellipsis "Ellipsis") of) *à la manière de*; in the manner of/in the style of[[1]](#cite_note-1)

[à fond](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%C3%A0_fond "wikt:à fond")
:   lit. "to the bottom"; thoroughly, completely, with maximum effort or intensity. Often used to describe giving one's all in a particular endeavor, as in "going à fond" in sports or other activities, or as in discussion "we discussed the subject à fond".

[à la carte](/wiki/%C3%80_la_carte "À la carte")
:   lit. "on the card, i.e. menu". In restaurants it refers to ordering individual dishes from the menu rather than a fixed-price meal. In America "à la carte menu" can be found, an [oxymoron](/wiki/Oxymoron "Oxymoron") and a [pleonasm](/wiki/Pleonasm "Pleonasm").

[à propos](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%C3%A0_propos "wiktionary:à propos")
:   regarding/concerning (the correct French is *à propos de*)

affaire de cœur
:   lit. a love affair

[aide-de-camp](/wiki/Aide-de-camp "Aide-de-camp")
:   lit. "camp helper"; "camp assistant", a military officer who serves as an adjutant to a higher-ranking officer, prince or other political dignitary. In Canada, it may also refer to the honorary position a person holds as a personal assistant to a high civil servant. In French, it is written *aide de camp* (without any hyphens).

[aide-mémoire](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/aide-m%C3%A9moire "wikt:aide-mémoire")
:   lit. "memory aid"; an object or memorandum to help one to remember things, or a diplomatic paper proposing the major points of discussion.

[amour propre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/amour_propre "wikt:amour propre")
:   "Self-love", self-respect.

[amuse-bouche](/wiki/Amuse-bouche "Amuse-bouche") or amuse-gueule
:   lit. "mouth-amuser"; a single, bite-sized [hors d'œuvre](/wiki/Hors_d%27%C5%93uvre "Hors d'œuvre"). In France, the exact expression used is *amuse-gueule*, *gueule* being slang for mouth (*gueule* is the mouth of a carnivorous animal; when used to describe a human mouth, it is vulgar—akin to "gob"— although the expression itself is not vulgar). The expression refers to a small mouthful of food, served at the discretion of the chef before a meal as an hors d'oeuvre or between main courses.

[ancien régime](/wiki/Ancien_r%C3%A9gime "Ancien régime")
:   a sociopolitical or other system that no longer exists, an allusion to pre-revolutionary France (used with capital letters in French with this meaning: [Ancien Régime](/wiki/Ancien_R%C3%A9gime "Ancien Régime"))

[aperçu](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/aper%C3%A7u "wikt:aperçu")
:   preview; a first impression; initial insight.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Aperitif_for_five.jpg/250px-Aperitif_for_five.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aperitif_for_five.jpg)

[Apéritifs](/wiki/Ap%C3%A9ritif "Apéritif") with amuse-gueules

[apéritif](/wiki/Ap%C3%A9ritif "Apéritif") or aperitif
:   lit. [drink] that "opens" the appetite, a pre-meal drink.[[2]](#cite_note-2) In colloquial French, *un apéritif* is usually shortened to *un apéro*.

appellation contrôlée
:   supervised use of a name. For the conventional use of the term, see [Appellation d'origine contrôlée](/wiki/Appellation_d%27origine_contr%C3%B4l%C3%A9e "Appellation d'origine contrôlée").

appetence
:   1. A natural craving or desire 2. An attraction or affinity; from the French "appétence", derived from "appétit" (appetite). In French, this has a high [register](/wiki/Register_(sociolinguistics) "Register (sociolinguistics)") language.

[après moi, le déluge](/wiki/Apr%C3%A8s_moi,_le_d%C3%A9luge "Après moi, le déluge")
:   lit. "After me, the [flood](/wiki/Flood_myth "Flood myth")", a remark attributed to [Louis XV](/wiki/Louis_XV "Louis XV") of France, referring to the impending end of a functioning French monarchy and predicting the French Revolution. It is derived from [Madame de Pompadour](/wiki/Madame_de_Pompadour "Madame de Pompadour")'s *[après nous, le déluge](https://en.wikiquote.org/wiki/Madame_de_Pompadour#Sourced "wikiquote:Madame de Pompadour")*, "after us, the flood". The [Royal Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force") [No. 617 Squadron](/wiki/No._617_Squadron_RAF "No. 617 Squadron RAF"), famously known as the "Dambusters", uses this as its motto.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Striding_Edge.jpg/250px-Striding_Edge.jpg)](/wiki/File:Striding_Edge.jpg)

[Arête](/wiki/Ar%C3%AAte "Arête")

[arête](/wiki/Ar%C3%AAte "Arête")
:   a narrow ridge. In French, also fishbone; edge of a polyhedron or graph; bridge of the nose.

[armoire](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/armoire "wikt:armoire")
:   a type of cabinet; wardrobe.

[arrière-pensée](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/arri%C3%A8re-pens%C3%A9e "wikt:arrière-pensée")
:   ulterior motive; concealed thought, plan, or motive.

[Art Nouveau](/wiki/Art_Nouveau "Art Nouveau")
:   a style of decoration and architecture of the late 19th and early 20th centuries. It takes capitals in French (*Art Nouveau*).

[attaché](/wiki/Attach%C3%A9 "Attaché")
:   a person attached to an embassy; in French it is also the past participle of the verb *attacher* (= to fasten, to tighten, to be linked)

[attaque au fer](/wiki/Fencing_terms#Blade_Work "Fencing terms")
:   an attack on the opponent's blade in [fencing](/wiki/Fencing "Fencing"), e.g. beat, expulsion, pressure.

[au contraire](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/au_contraire "wikt:au contraire")
:   on the contrary.

[au courant](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/au_courant "wikt:au courant")
:   up-to-date; abreast of current affairs.

[au fait](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/au_fait "wikt:au fait")
:   being familiar with or know about something.

au gratin
:   "with gratings", anything that is grated onto a food dish. In English, specifically 'with cheese'.

[au jus](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/au_jus "wikt:au jus")
:   lit. "with juice", referring to a food course served with sauce. Often redundantly formulated, as in 'Open-faced steak sandwich, served with au jus.' No longer used in French, except for the colloquial, *être au jus* (to be informed).

au naturel
:   1. a. Nude. b. In a natural state: an *au naturel* hairstyle. 2. Cooked simply. Also used in French heraldry to mean "proper" i.e. in natural colours.

[au pair](/wiki/Au_pair "Au pair")
:   a young foreigner who does domestic chores in exchange for room and board. In France, those chores are mainly child care/education.

[au revoir](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/au_revoir "wikt:au revoir")!
:   "See you later!" In French, a contraction of *Au plaisir de vous revoir* ('to the pleasure of seeing you again').

[au sec](/w/index.php?title=Au_sec&action=edit&redlink=1 "Au sec (page does not exist)")
:   lit. "almost dry", reducing liquid to the point of almost being dry though the food is still moist.

[avant-garde](/wiki/Avant-garde "Avant-garde") (pl. *avant-gardes*)
:   applied to cutting-edge or radically innovative movements in art, music and literature; figuratively 'on the edge', literally, a military term, meaning 'vanguard' (which is a corruption of *avant-garde*) or "advance guard", in other words, "first to attack" (antonym of *arrière-garde*).

avant la lettre
:   used to describe something or someone seen as a forerunner of something (such as an artistic or political movement) before that thing was recognized and named, e.g., "a post-modernist avant la lettre", "a feminist avant la lettre". The expression literally means "before the letter", i.e., "before it had a name".[[3]](#cite_note-3) An alternative modern French version of this expression is *avant l'heure*.

[avoirdupois](/wiki/Avoirdupois "Avoirdupois")
:   used in Middle English, *avoir de pois* = commodities sold by weight, alteration of Old French *aveir de peis* = "goods of weight". In Modern French, only used to refer to English weight measures, as in *une livre avoirdupois* (1 lb. avdp) as opposed to *une livre troy* (1 lb. troy).

### B

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Glossary_of_French_words_and_expressions_in_English&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: B")]

[baguette](/wiki/Baguette "Baguette")
:   a long, narrow loaf of white bread with a crisp crust, often called "French bread" or "French stick" in the United Kingdom. In French, a *baguette* is any long and narrow stick-like object such as a chopstick; a rectangular diamond cut to 25 facets and a magic wand.

[banquette](/wiki/Banquette "Banquette")
:   a long upholstered bench or a sofa.

[beaucoup de](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/beaucoup_de "wiktionary:beaucoup de")
:   Used interchangeably with the English equivalent of "lots of/many/a great number of". Appropriate when the speaker wants to convey a greater positive connotation and/or greater emphasis. Often used as an informal expression, mostly in small regional dialect-pockets in the Canadian Prairies and the American South, especially in Alberta and Louisiana respectively.

[Beau geste](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/beau_geste "wiktionary:beau geste")
:   lit. "beautiful gesture", a gracious gesture, noble in form but often futile or meaningless in substance. This French expression has been on the periphery of the standard English lexicon since the appearance of [P. C. Wren](/wiki/P._C._Wren "P. C. Wren")'s *[Beau Geste](/wiki/Beau_Geste "Beau Geste")* (1924), the first of his Foreign Legion novels.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

[Beau idéal](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/beau_id%C3%A9al "wiktionary:beau idéal")
:   lit. "beautiful ideal," used to suggest the perfect or most supreme version of something to exist. The expression was coined during the late 18th century during the aesthetic period known as [classicism](/wiki/Classicism "Classicism"). Invoking the balance and refinement of Greek and Roman art and architecture, the term was used for examples of such that conformed to purity, wholesomeness, equilibrium, and simple elegance.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

[Beaux-Arts](/wiki/Beaux_arts_architecture "Beaux arts architecture")
:   monumental architectural style of the early 20th century made famous by the [Académie des Beaux-Arts](/wiki/Acad%C3%A9mie_des_Beaux-Arts "Académie des Beaux-Arts").

[bel esprit](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/bel_esprit "wiktionary:bel esprit") (pl. beaux esprits)
:   lit. "fine mind"; a cultivated, highly intelligent person.

[Belle Époque](/wiki/Belle_%C3%89poque "Belle Époque")
:   a period in European social history that began during the late 19th century and lasted until World War I.

[belles-lettres](/wiki/Belles-lettres "Belles-lettres")
:   lit. "fine letters"; literature regarded for its aesthetic value rather than its didactic or informative content; also, light, stylish writings, usually on literary or intellectual subjects.

[bien entendu](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/bien_entendu "wiktionary:bien entendu")
:   well understood, well known, obvious – "of course".

[bien pensant](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/bien_pensant "wiktionary:bien pensant")
:   lit. "well thinking"; right thinking, orthodox. Formerly implied wilful blindness to dangers or suffering faced by others but, nowadays corresponds to "[politically correct](/wiki/Political_correctness "Political correctness")". The noun form *bien-pensance* is rarely seen in English.

billet-doux
:   lit. "sweet note", love letter[[6]](#cite_note-6)

[blasé](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/blas%C3%A9 "wiktionary:blasé")
:   unimpressed with something because of overfamiliarity, jaded.

[bon appétit](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/bon_app%C3%A9tit "wiktionary:bon appétit")
:   lit. "good appetite"; "enjoy your meal".

[bon mot](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/bon_mot "wiktionary:bon mot") (pl. *bons mots*)
:   well-chosen word(s), particularly a witty remark ("each bon mot which falls from his lips is analysed and filed away for posterity", *The European Magazine*, August 29 – September 4, 1996)

[bon vivant](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/bon_vivant "wiktionary:bon vivant")
:   one who enjoys the good life, an [epicurean](/wiki/Epicureanism "Epicureanism").

[bon voyage](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/bon_voyage "wiktionary:bon voyage")

[boudoir](/wiki/Boudoir "Boudoir")
:   lit. "sulking place"; a woman's private dressing or sitting room in a house.

[bourgeois](/wiki/Bourgeois "Bourgeois")
:   member of the [bourgeoisie](/wiki/Bourgeoisie "Bourgeoisie"), originally councilmen, burghers or even aristocrats living in towns in the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"). Now the term is derogatory, and it applies to a person whose beliefs, attitudes, and practices are conventionally middle-class.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Bric-%C3%A0-brac_shop_at_Nantucket.jpg/250px-Bric-%C3%A0-brac_shop_at_Nantucket.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bric-%C3%A0-brac_shop_at_Nantucket.jpg)

[Bric-à-brac](/wiki/Bric-%C3%A0-brac "Bric-à-brac")

[bric-à-brac](/wiki/Bric-%C3%A0-brac "Bric-à-brac")
:   small ornamental objects, less valuable than antiques; a collection of old furniture, china, plates and curiosities. Cf. *de bric et de broc*, corresponding to English "by hook or by crook", and *brack*, refuse.

[bricolage](/wiki/Bricolage "Bricolage")
:   to improvise or assemble something useful from what happens to be at hand; to expedite or economize a project with readily available components, versus a kit or outside sources; to reuse spare parts for other than their original purpose; to create something new by arranging old material; to create a new, valuable purpose for an object that has completed its original purpose and would otherwise be discarded. Connotes an intrepid [do-it-yourself](/wiki/DIY "DIY") spirit or clever repurposing. Differs from tinkering which merely modifies an existing arrangement. The term is used metaphorically to describe inventive philosophy, theories, and practices in business and academic fields, where new concepts are found in interactions of old ideas.

[brioche](/wiki/Brioche "Brioche")

[bureau](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/bureau "wikt:bureau") (pl. *bureaux*)
:   government office; an agency for information exchange. Also means "desk" in French, and in the U.K.

:   "look for / seek the woman", in the sense that, when a man behaves out of character or in an otherwise apparently inexplicable manner, the reason may be found in his trying to cover up an illicit affair with a woman, or to impress or gain favour with a woman. This expression was first used in a novel by [Alexandre Dumas (père)](/wiki/Alexandre_Dumas,_p%C3%A8re "Alexandre Dumas, père"), in the third chapter of *Les Mohicans de Paris* (1854), in the form of *cherchons la femme* ("let's look for the woman"). The expression is found in John Latey's 1878 English translation: "Ah! Monsieur Jackal, you were right when you said, 'Seek the woman.'" The phrase was adopted into everyday English use and crossed the Atlantic by 1909.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

entente

[entre nous](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/entre_nous "wiktionary:entre nous")

[entrée](/wiki/Entr%C3%A9e "Entrée")

[entrepreneur](/wiki/Entrepreneur "Entrepreneur")

[épater la bourgeoisie](/wiki/%C3%89pater_la_bourgeoisie "Épater la bourgeoisie") or épater le bourgeois

[escargot](/wiki/Escargot "Escargot")

l'État, c'est moi!

[étude](/wiki/%C3%89tude "Étude")

[étui](/wiki/%C3%89tui "Étui")

[excusez-moi](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/excusez-moi "wiktionary:excusez-moi")

extraordinaire

faites comme chez vous

[faux](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/faux "wikt:faux")

faux pas

fiancé(e)

fils

fin de siècle
:   The end of the century, a term which typically encompasses both the meaning of the similar English idiom [turn of the century](/wiki/Turn_of_the_century "Turn of the century") and also makes reference to the closing of one era and onset of another.

[flambé](/wiki/Flamb%C3%A9 "Flambé")

flambeau

Is one of the more expensive salts; traditional French fleur de sel is collected off the coast of [Brittany](/wiki/Brittany "Brittany") most notably in the town of [Guérande](/wiki/Gu%C3%A9rande "Guérande") (*Fleur de Sel de Guérande* being the most revered), but also in [Noirmoutier](/wiki/Noirmoutier "Noirmoutier"), [Île de Ré](/wiki/%C3%8Ele_de_R%C3%A9 "Île de Ré") and [Camargue](/wiki/Camargue "Camargue").

habitué

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Robe_lacroix_arles_2008.jpg/250px-Robe_lacroix_arles_2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Robe_lacroix_arles_2008.jpg)

### J

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Glossary_of_French_words_and_expressions_in_English&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: J")]

j'accuse
:   "I accuse"; used generally in reference to a political or social indictment (alluding to [J'Accuse…!](/wiki/J%27Accuse%E2%80%A6! "J'Accuse…!"), [Émile Zola](/wiki/%C3%89mile_Zola "Émile Zola")'s exposé of the [Dreyfus affair](/wiki/Dreyfus_affair "Dreyfus affair"), a political scandal that divided France from the 1890s to the early 1900s (decade) and involved the false conviction for treason in 1894 of [Alfred Dreyfus](/wiki/Alfred_Dreyfus "Alfred Dreyfus"), a young French artillery officer of Jewish background).

j'adoube
:   In [chess](/wiki/Chess "Chess"), an expression, said discreetly, that signals the intention to straighten the pieces without committing to move or capturing the first one touched as per the game's rules; lit. "I adjust", from *adouber*, to dub (the action of knighting someone).

je ne regrette rien
:   "I regret nothing" (from the title of a popular song sung by [Édith Piaf](/wiki/%C3%89dith_Piaf "Édith Piaf"): *[Non, je ne regrette rien](/wiki/Non,_je_ne_regrette_rien "Non, je ne regrette rien")*). Also the phrase the UK's then [Chancellor of the Exchequer](/wiki/Chancellor_of_the_Exchequer "Chancellor of the Exchequer") [Norman Lamont](/wiki/Norman_Lamont_(1942) "Norman Lamont (1942)") chose to use to describe his feelings over the events of September 16, 1992 ('[Black Wednesday](/wiki/Black_Wednesday "Black Wednesday")').

[je ne sais quoi](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/je_ne_sais_quoi "wiktionary:je ne sais quoi")
:   lit. "I-don't-know-what": an indescribable or indefinable 'something' that distinguishes the object in question from others that are superficially similar.

[jeu d'esprit](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/jeu_d%27esprit "wiktionary:jeu d'esprit")
:   lit. "play of spirit": a witty, often light-hearted, comment or composition

[jeunesse dorée](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/jeunesse_dor%C3%A9e "wiktionary:jeunesse dorée")

"gilded youth"; name given to a body of young [dandies](/wiki/Dandy "Dandy"), also called the [Muscadins](/wiki/Muscadin "Muscadin"), who, after the fall of [Robespierre](/wiki/Maximilien_Robespierre "Maximilien Robespierre"), fought against the [Jacobins](/wiki/Jacobin_(politics) "Jacobin (politics)"). Today used for youthful offspring, particularly if bullying and vandalistic, of the affluent.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

[joie de vivre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/joie_de_vivre "wiktionary:joie de vivre")

### L

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Glossary_of_French_words_and_expressions_in_English&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: L")]

[l'appel du vide](/wiki/L%27appel_du_vide "L'appel du vide")
:   lit. "call of the void"; used to refer to intellectual suicidal thoughts, or the urge to engage in self-destructive (suicidal) behaviors during everyday life. Examples include thinking about swerving in to the opposite lane while driving, or feeling the urge to jump off a cliff edge while standing on it. These thoughts are not accompanied by emotional distress.

[laissez-faire](/wiki/Laissez-faire "Laissez-faire")
:   lit. "let do"; often used within the context of [economic policy](/wiki/Economic_policy "Economic policy") or [political philosophy](/wiki/Political_philosophy "Political philosophy"), meaning leaving alone, or non-interference. The phrase is the shortcut of *[Laissez faire, laissez passer](/wiki/Laissez_faire,_laissez_passer "Laissez faire, laissez passer")*, a doctrine first supported by the [Physiocrats](/wiki/Physiocrats "Physiocrats") in the 18th century. The motto was invented by [Vincent de Gournay](/wiki/Vincent_de_Gournay "Vincent de Gournay"), and it became popular among supporters of [free trade](/wiki/Free-trade "Free-trade") and [economic liberalism](/wiki/Economic_liberalism "Economic liberalism"). It is also used to describe a parental style in developmental psychology, where the parent(s) does not apply rules or guiding. As per the parental style, it is now one of the major management styles. Used more generally in modern English to describe a particularly casual or "hands-off" attitude or approach to something,

[laissez-passer](/wiki/Laissez-Passer "Laissez-Passer")
:   a travel document, a passport

[laissez les bons temps rouler](/wiki/Laissez_les_bons_temps_rouler "Laissez les bons temps rouler")
:   [Cajun](/wiki/Cajun "Cajun") expression for "let the good times roll": not used in proper French, and not generally understood by Francophones outside [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana "Louisiana"), who would say *profitez des bons moments* (enjoy the good moments).

[lamé](/wiki/Lam%C3%A9_(fabric) "Lamé (fabric)")
:   a type of fabric woven or knit with metallic yarns.

[lanterne rouge](/wiki/Lanterne_rouge "Lanterne rouge")
:   the last-place finisher in a cycling stage race; most commonly used in connection with the Tour de France.

[lèse majesté](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/l%C3%A8se_majest%C3%A9 "wiktionary:lèse majesté")
:   an offense against a sovereign power; or, an attack against someone's dignity or against a custom or institution held sacred (from the Latin *crimen laesae maiestatis*: the crime of injured majesty).

liaison
:   a close relationship or connection; an affair. The French meaning is broader; *liaison* also means "bond"' such as in *une liaison chimique* (a chemical bond)

[lingerie](/wiki/Lingerie "Lingerie")
:   a type of female underwear.

[littérateur](/wiki/Litt%C3%A9rateur "Littérateur")
:   an intellectual (can be pejorative in French, meaning someone who writes a lot but does not have a particular skill).[[37]](#cite_note-37)

louche

Louis Quatorze
:   ["Louis XIV" (of France)](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV"), the Sun King, usually a reference to décor or furniture design.

Louis Quinze
:   ["Louis XV" (of France)](/wiki/Louis_XV "Louis XV"), associated with the [rococo](/wiki/Rococo "Rococo") style of furniture, architecture and interior decoration.

[Mardi Gras](/wiki/Mardi_Gras "Mardi Gras")

[Mardi Gras](/wiki/Mardi_Gras "Mardi Gras")
:   Fat Tuesday or Shrove Tuesday, the last day of eating meat, eggs and fatty foods before [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent").

[marque](/wiki/Marque "Marque")
:   a model or brand.

[matériel](/wiki/Mat%C3%A9riel "Matériel")
:   supplies and equipment, particularly in a military context (French meaning is broader and corresponds more to "hardware")

[mauvais quart d'heure](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/mauvais_quart_d%27heure "wiktionary:mauvais quart d'heure")
:   lit. "bad quarter hour": a short unpleasant or uncomfortable moment.

[mdr](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/mort_de_rire "wiktionary:mort de rire")
:   Alt., MDR. Abbreviation in SMS, [akin to LOL](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/mort_de_rire "wikt:mort de rire"); for *mort de rire* (*mort*, adj. or verb, past tense), or *mourir de rire* (*mourir*, verb, infinitive). Lit., as adjective or past tense, dead or [died](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/die "wikt:die") [of laughing](/wiki/Laugh "Laugh"), so "died laughing" or "dying of laughter"; compare *mort de faim* for starve.

[mélange](/wiki/M%C3%A9lange "Mélange")
:   a mixture.

[mêlée](/wiki/M%C3%AAl%C3%A9e "Mêlée")
:   a confused fight; a struggling crowd. In French also: a rugby scrum.

[ménage à trois](/wiki/M%C3%A9nage_%C3%A0_trois "Ménage à trois")
:   lit. "household for three": a sexual arrangement between three people.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

[ménagerie](/wiki/Menagerie "Menagerie")
:   a collection of wild animals kept in captivity for exhibition.

[métier](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/m%C3%A9tier "wiktionary:métier")
:   a field of work or other activity; usually one in which one has special ability or training.

[milieu](/wiki/Social_environment "Social environment")
:   social environment; setting (has also the meaning of "middle", and organized crime community in French).

[milieu intérieur](/wiki/Milieu_int%C3%A9rieur "Milieu intérieur")
:   the extra-cellular fluid environment, and its physiological capacity to ensure protective stability for the tissues and organs of multicellular living organisms.

[mirepoix](/wiki/Mirepoix_(cuisine) "Mirepoix (cuisine)")
:   a cooking mixture of two parts onions and one part each of celery and carrots.

[mise en place](/wiki/Mise_en_place "Mise en place")
:   an assembly of ingredients, usually set up in small bowls, used to facilitate cooking. This means all the raw ingredients are prepared and ready to go before cooking. Translated, "put in place."

[mise en abyme](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/mise_en_abyme "wiktionary:mise en abyme")
:   lit. "placed into abyss": a literary and artistic technique where a work contains a smaller copy of itself, creating a potentially infinite recursive loop. The term can describe a story within a story, a play within a play, or any self-referential artistic device.

[mise en scène](/wiki/Mise_en_sc%C3%A8ne "Mise en scène")
:   the process of setting a stage with regard to placement of actors, scenery, properties, etc.; the stage setting or scenery of a play; surroundings, environment.

[mise en table](/w/index.php?title=Mise_en_table&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mise en table (page does not exist)")
:   table setting.

[montage](/wiki/Montage_(filmmaking) "Montage (filmmaking)")
:   editing.

le [mot juste](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/mot_juste "wiktionary:mot juste")
:   lit. "the just word"; the right word at the right time. French uses it often in the expression *chercher le mot juste* (to search for the right word).

[motif](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/motif "wikt:motif")
:   a recurrent thematic element.

[moue](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/moue "wiktionary:moue")
:   a type of [facial expression](/wiki/Facial_expression "Facial expression"); pursing together of the lips to indicate dissatisfaction, a [pout](/wiki/Pout_(disambiguation) "Pout (disambiguation)"). See [snout reflex](/wiki/Snout_reflex "Snout reflex").

[mousse](/wiki/Mousse "Mousse")
:   a whipped dessert or a hairstyling foam; in French, however, it refers to any type of foam or moss.

### N

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Glossary_of_French_words_and_expressions_in_English&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: N")]

[naïveté](/wiki/Na%C3%AFvet%C3%A9 "Naïveté")
:   Lack of sophistication, experience, judgement, or worldliness; artlessness; gullibility; credulity.

[né](/wiki/N%C3%A9 "Né"), [née](/wiki/N%C3%A9e "Née")
:   lit. "born": a man's/woman's birth name ([maiden name](/wiki/Married_and_maiden_names "Married and maiden names") for a woman), e.g., "Martha Washington, *née* Dandridge."

[n'est-ce pas?](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/n%27est-ce_pas "wikt:n'est-ce pas")
:   "isn't it [true]?"
:   asked rhetorically after a statement, as in "Right?".

[noblesse oblige](/wiki/Noblesse_oblige "Noblesse oblige")
:   "nobility obliges"
:   those granted a higher station in life have a duty to extend (possibly token) favours/courtesies to those in lower stations.

[nom de guerre](/wiki/Pseudonym "Pseudonym")
:   pseudonym to disguise the identity of a leader of a militant group, literally "war name", used in France for "pseudonym".[[42]](#cite_note-42)

[nom de plume](/wiki/Pen_name "Pen name")
:   a "back-translation" from the English "pen name": author's pseudonym.
:   Although now used in French as well, the term was coined in English by analogy with *nom de guerre*.

[nonpareil](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/nonpareil "wikt:nonpareil")
:   Unequalled, unrivalled; unparalleled; unique
:   the modern French equivalent of this expression is *sans pareil* (literally "without equal").

[nouveau](/wiki/Nouveau "Nouveau") (pl. nouveaux; fem. nouvelle; fem. pl. nouvelles)
:   new.

[nouveau riche](/wiki/Nouveau_riche "Nouveau riche")
:   lit. "newly rich"
:   used to refer particularly to those living a garish lifestyle with their newfound wealth; see also [arriviste](/wiki/Arriviste "Arriviste") and [parvenu](/wiki/Parvenu "Parvenu").

[nouvelle vague](/wiki/Nouvelle_vague "Nouvelle vague")
:   lit. "new wave."
:   Used for stating a new way or a new trend of something. Originally marked a new style of French filmmaking in the late 1950s and early 1960s, reacting against films seen as too literary.

### O

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Glossary_of_French_words_and_expressions_in_English&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: O")]

[objet d'art](/wiki/Objet_d%27art "Objet d'art")
:   a work of art, commonly a painting or sculpture; also a utilitarian object displayed for its aesthetic qualities

[œuvre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%C5%93uvre "wiktionary:œuvre")
:   "work", in the sense of an artist's work; by extension, an artist's entire body of work.

[opéra bouffe](/wiki/Op%C3%A9ra_bouffe "Opéra bouffe")
:   musical comedy, satire, parody or farce.

[oubliette](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/oubliette "wiktionary:oubliette")
:   "little forgotten place": a type of dungeon accessible only from a hatch or hole in a high ceiling, making escape nearly impossible.

[outré](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/outr%C3%A9 "wikt:outré")
:   exceeding the lines of propriety; eccentric in behavior or appearance in an inappropriate way

### P

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Glossary_of_French_words_and_expressions_in_English&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: P")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Pain_au_chocolat_Luc_Viatour.jpg/250px-Pain_au_chocolat_Luc_Viatour.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pain_au_chocolat_Luc_Viatour.jpg)

[Pain au chocolat](/wiki/Pain_au_chocolat "Pain au chocolat")

[pain au chocolat](/wiki/Pain_au_chocolat "Pain au chocolat")
:   lit. "bread with chocolate." Despite the name, it is not made of bread but [puff pastry](/wiki/Puff_pastry "Puff pastry") with chocolate inside. The term *chocolatine* is used in some Francophone areas (especially the South-West) and sometimes in English.

[pain aux raisins](/wiki/Pain_aux_raisins "Pain aux raisins")
:   raisin bread.

[panache](/wiki/Panache "Panache")
:   verve; flamboyance.

[papier-mâché](/wiki/Papier-m%C3%A2ch%C3%A9 "Papier-mâché")
:   lit. "chewed paper"; a craft medium using paper and paste.

[par avion](/wiki/Par_avion "Par avion")
:   by aircraft. In English, specifically by air mail, from the phrase found on air mail envelopes.

[par excellence](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/par_excellence "wiktionary:par excellence")
:   better than all the others, quintessential.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

[parc fermé](/wiki/Parc_ferm%C3%A9 "Parc fermé")
:   lit. "closed park". A secure area at a [Grand Prix](/wiki/Grand_Prix_motor_racing "Grand Prix motor racing") circuit where the cars may be stored overnight.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Daniel_Ilabaca.jpg/250px-Daniel_Ilabaca.jpg)](/wiki/File:Daniel_Ilabaca.jpg)

[Parkour](/wiki/Parkour "Parkour")

[parkour](/wiki/Parkour "Parkour")
:   urban street sport involving climbing and leaping, using buildings, walls, curbs to ricochet off much as if one were on a skateboard, often in follow-the-leader style. Originally a phonetic form of the French word *parcours*, which means "a run, a route" Also known as, or the predecessor to, "free running", developed by [Sébastien Foucan](/wiki/S%C3%A9bastien_Foucan "Sébastien Foucan").

**parole**
:   1) (in linguistics) speech, more specifically the individual, personal phenomenon of language; see [langue and parole](/wiki/Langue_and_parole "Langue and parole"). 2) (in criminal justice) conditional early release from prison; see [parole](/wiki/Parole "Parole").

[parvenu](/wiki/Parvenu "Parvenu")
:   a social upstart.

[pas de deux](/wiki/Pas_de_deux "Pas de deux")
:   lit. "step for two"; in ballet, a dance or figure for two performers, a duet; also a close relationship between two people.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

[pas de trois](/wiki/Pas_de_trois "Pas de trois")
:   lit. "step for three"; in ballet, a dance or figure for three performers.

[passe-partout](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/passe-partout "wiktionary:passe-partout")
:   a document or key that allows the holder to travel without hindrance from the authorities or enter any location.

[pastiche](/wiki/Pastiche "Pastiche")
:   a derivative work; an imitation.

[patois](/wiki/Patois "Patois")
:   a dialect; jargon.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

[peloton](/wiki/Peloton "Peloton")
:   in [road cycling](/wiki/Road_bicycle_racing "Road bicycle racing"), the main group of riders in a race.

**penchant**

> inclination; decided taste; bias. "a penchant for art".

[père](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/p%C3%A8re "wiktionary:père")
:   lit. "father", used after a man's surname to distinguish a father from a son, as in [Alexandre Dumas, père](/wiki/Alexandre_Dumas,_p%C3%A8re "Alexandre Dumas, père").

[petit pois](/wiki/Pea "Pea")
:   small peas, often sold in the frozen food aisle.

[petite bourgeoisie](/wiki/Petite_bourgeoisie "Petite bourgeoisie")
:   often anglicised as "petty bourgeoisie", used to designate the middle class.

[la petite mort](/wiki/La_petite_mort "La petite mort")
:   lit. "the little death"; an expression for the weakening or loss of consciousness following an intense [orgasm](/wiki/Orgasm "Orgasm").

[Pied-Noir](/wiki/Pied-Noir "Pied-Noir") (plural Pieds-Noirs)
:   lit. "black foot", a European [Algerian](/wiki/Algeria "Algeria") in the pre-independence state.

[pied-à-terre](/wiki/Pied-%C3%A0-terre "Pied-à-terre") (also pied à terre)
:   lit. "foot-on-the-ground"; a place to stay, generally small and applied to a secondary residence in a city.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/T_Roosevelt.jpg/250px-T_Roosevelt.jpg)](/wiki/File:T_Roosevelt.jpg)

[Pince-nez](/wiki/Pince-nez "Pince-nez")

[pince-nez](/wiki/Pince-nez "Pince-nez")
:   lit. "nose-pincher", a type of spectacles without temple arms.

[piste](/wiki/Piste "Piste")
:   lit. "trail" or "track"; often used referring to skiing at a ski area (on piste) versus skiing in the back country (off piste).

[plage](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/plage "wiktionary:plage")
:   beach, especially a fashionable seaside resort.

plat du jour
:   lit. "dish of the day"; a dish served in a restaurant on a particular day but separate from the regular menu.

[plongeur](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/plongeur "wiktionary:plongeur") (fem. plongeuse)
:   a male (or female) dishwasher in a professional kitchen.

[plus ça change, plus c'est la même chose](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/plus_%C3%A7a_change,_plus_c%27est_la_m%C3%AAme_chose "wiktionary:plus ça change, plus c'est la même chose") (or *plus ça change, plus c'est pareil*) (often abbreviated to just *plus ça change*)
:   the more things change, the more they stay the same. An aphorism coined by [Jean-Baptiste Alphonse Karr](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste_Alphonse_Karr "Jean-Baptiste Alphonse Karr").

[point d'appui](/wiki/Point_d%27appui "Point d'appui")
:   a location where troops assemble prior to a battle. While this figurative meaning also exists in French, the first and literal meaning of *point d'appui* is a fixed point from which a person or thing executes a movement (such as a footing in climbing or a pivot).

[porte-cochère](/wiki/Porte-coch%C3%A8re "Porte-cochère")
:   an architectural term referring to a doorway, sometimes ornate, intended for the passage of vehicles. Literally a "coach door". Written in French without the hyphen : *porte cochère*

[poseur](/wiki/Poseur "Poseur")
:   lit. "poser": a person who pretends to be something he is not; an affected or insincere person; a wannabe.

[pot-au-feu](/wiki/Pot-au-feu "Pot-au-feu")
:   stew, soup.

pour encourager les autres
:   lit. "to encourage others"; said of an excessive punishment meted out as an example, to deter others. The original is from [Voltaire](/wiki/Voltaire "Voltaire")'s *[Candide](/wiki/Candide "Candide")* and referred to the execution of Admiral [John Byng](/wiki/John_Byng "John Byng").[[47]](#cite_note-47)

[pourboire](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/pourboire "wiktionary:pourboire")
:   lit. "for drink"; gratuity, tip; *donner un pourboire*: to tip.

[prairie](/wiki/Prairie "Prairie")
:   lit. "meadow"; expansive natural meadows of long grass.

[prêt-à-porter](/wiki/Pr%C3%AAt-%C3%A0-porter "Prêt-à-porter")
:   lit. "ready to wear"; clothing off the shelf, in contrast to *[haute couture](/wiki/Haute_couture "Haute couture")*.

[prie-dieu](/wiki/Prie-dieu "Prie-dieu")
:   lit. "pray [to] God"; a type of prayer desk.

[prix fixe](/wiki/Prix_fixe "Prix fixe")
:   lit. "fixed price"; a menu on which multi-course meals with only a few choices are charged at a fixed price.

[protégé](/wiki/Mentorship "Mentorship") (fem. protégée)
:   lit. "protected"; a man/woman who receives support from an influential mentor.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

[provocateur](/wiki/Polemic "Polemic")
:   an agitator, a polemicist.

[purée](/wiki/Pur%C3%A9e "Purée")
:   lit. a smooth, creamy substance made of liquidized or crushed fruit or vegetables.

quelle [bonne](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/bonne "wikt:bonne") [idée](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/id%C3%A9e "wikt:idée")!

quelle [horreur](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/horreur "wikt:horreur")!
:   *What a horrible thing!* (can be used sarcastically).

quelle surprise!

### R

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Glossary_of_French_words_and_expressions_in_English&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: R")]

[raconteur](/wiki/Raconteur "Raconteur")
:   a storyteller.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

[raison d'être](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/raison_d%27%C3%AAtre "wikt:raison d'être")
:   "reason for being": justification or purpose of existence.

[rapprochement](/wiki/Rapprochement "Rapprochement")
:   the establishment of cordial relations, often used in [diplomacy](/wiki/Diplomacy "Diplomacy").[[50]](#cite_note-50)

[reconnaissance](/wiki/Reconnaissance "Reconnaissance")
:   scouting, the military exploration outside an area that friendly forces occupy

[recueillement](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/recueillement "wikt:recueillement")
:   lit. "gathering" or "collecting oneself"; a state of quiet contemplation, meditation, or introspective reflection; spiritual or mental withdrawal for the purpose of prayer, thought, or reverence.

[refoulement](/wiki/Refoulement "Refoulement")
:   the expulsion of persons who have the right to be recognised as refugees.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

[Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance")
:   a historical period or cultural movement of rebirth

[reportage](/wiki/Reportage "Reportage")
:   reporting; journalism.

répondez s'il-vous-plaît. ([RSVP](/wiki/RSVP_(invitations) "RSVP (invitations)"))
:   *Please reply*. Though francophones may use more usually "prière de répondre" or "je vous prie de bien vouloir répondre", it is common enough.

[restaurateur](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/restaurateur "wikt:restaurateur")
:   a restaurant owner.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

[revenant](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/revenant "wiktionary:revenant")
:   lit. "one who returns": a person who returns from a long absence or from the dead; a ghost or apparition of a deceased person that is believed to have returned from the grave to terrorize the living. From the French verb *revenir* (to return). In folklore and gothic literature, revenants are typically corporeal undead beings, distinct from ethereal spirits, who return with a specific purpose such as revenge or to complete unfinished business. The term has been adopted in modern English to describe anyone who returns after a prolonged absence, particularly in dramatic or unexpected circumstances.

[Rive Gauche](/wiki/Rive_Gauche "Rive Gauche")
:   the left (southern) bank (of the [River Seine](/wiki/River_Seine "River Seine") in Paris). A particular mindset attributed to inhabitants of that area, which includes the [Sorbonne](/wiki/Sorbonne_(building) "Sorbonne (building)")

roi fainéant
:   lit. "do-nothing king": an expression first used about the [kings of France](/wiki/King_of_France "King of France") from 670 to 752 (Thierry III to Childeric III), who were puppets of their ministers. The term was later used about other royalty who had been made powerless, also in other countries, but lost its meaning when [parliamentarism](/wiki/Parliamentarism "Parliamentarism") made all royals powerless.
